resumen: A dead grandmother's old TV receives a channel that turns grief into an endless sitcom.
contenido: My grandmother died watching a TV channel that didn't exist.

That's what the police report said, although in different words. They found her sitting in her recliner at her house in Nebraska, a blanket over her legs, a cold cup of tea on the table, and her eyes wide open in front of the television.
The screen was off.
But the neighbors swore that all night long, they heard people laughing from her living room.
Not normal laughter.
A laugh track, like the kind from old '90s sitcoms.
When I came to clean out the house, I found the remote control resting on the arm of the recliner. There was a piece of tape stuck over one button.
The number 9.

My grandmother didn't have cable. She used an old antenna on the roof, the kind you still see on rural houses, leaning into the wind like a metal skeleton. It only picked up three local channels: news, weather, and a religious station that aired sermons on Sundays.
But in a notebook beside the phone, I found a note written in her shaky handwriting:
"Do not watch Channel 9 after midnight. They know when you're alone."
I thought it was a sign of dementia. My grandmother had been forgetting names for months, leaving the oven on, calling me by my dead father's name.
That night, I stayed at her house to finish packing.
That was a mistake.
At 12:09 a.m., the TV turned on by itself.
First, there was static.
Then a living room appeared.
Not just any living room.
My grandmother's living room.
The same green carpet, the same floral curtains, the same wooden clock on the wall.
But on the screen, there was a family sitting on the couch.
A father in a plaid shirt.
A mother in a yellow dress.
Two blond kids.
All of them smiling too much.
At the bottom of the screen, the title of the show appeared:
"The Happy House on County Road 6."
Then I heard applause.
It wasn't only coming from the TV.
It was coming from behind me.
I turned around.
The house was empty.
When I looked back at the screen, the mother on the show was staring straight into the camera.
Straight at me.
She smiled and said:
"Tonight's special guest is already here."
I tried to turn off the TV.
It didn't respond.
I took the batteries out of the remote. Nothing. I unplugged the cord from the wall, and the image stayed alive, lighting the room with a sick blue glow.
The family on the screen started acting like I was part of the episode.
The father looked off to the side, where the audience should have been, and said:
"Looks like Daniel still doesn't understand the house rules."
The laugh track exploded.
I froze.
I had never told them my name.
The mother lifted an empty coffee mug.
"Rule number one: nobody changes the channel."
More laughter.
One of the kids, the younger one, leaned toward the camera.
"Rule number two: if someone sits in Grandma's chair, they stay for next season."
I looked at the recliner.
My grandmother's blanket was still there.
But now it had the shape of someone underneath it.
Very slowly, the blanket moved.
Not like a person breathing.
Like something practicing how to.
I ran to the kitchen and called 911. The line rang once, twice, and then a cheerful intro theme started playing, with saxophone and applause. An announcer's voice said:
"Previously on Channel 9..."
The phone screen showed a video.
My grandmother sitting in her recliner the night before.
Watching TV.
Crying.
In the video, the family from the show was talking to her from the screen.
"Just say you want to stay," the mother said. "Nobody has to die alone."
My grandmother shook her head.
Then the father looked out of the television and said:
"We can always use the family."
The signal cut out.
From the living room, I heard my grandmother's voice.
"Danny, come here, sweetheart."
I didn't move.
My grandmother never called me Danny.

She called me mijo, even though we weren't Latino. She had learned it from a Mexican neighbor who used to help her with groceries, and after that she used it with me because she said it sounded warmer.
I walked slowly back into the living room.
On the TV, the family was sitting at the dinner table. There was an empty plate at the head of the table. On it, written in red sauce, was my name.
The hallway door opened by itself.
At the end of the hall, in my grandmother's bedroom, a light flickered like the light on a TV set.
I went in with a flashlight.

The walls were covered in old photos. But they weren't family photos. They were screenshots. People sitting in recliners, beds, kitchens, all watching TV in the middle of the night. Some photos were from the seventies. Others looked recent.
In every one of them, the Channel 9 family appeared somewhere in the background, reflected in a screen.
And in one of the photos was my father.
My father had died in a car accident when I was nine.
In the picture, he was sitting in the same house, watching the same TV, with the same empty expression my grandmother had when they found her.
Behind me, the door closed.
The bedroom TV turned on.
My father appeared on the screen.
Not the way I remembered him dead, but young, healthy, wearing his Cornhuskers jacket and that crooked smile I had spent years trying not to forget.
"Hey, buddy," he said. "Come watch with me."
I felt my chest break.
The image was perfect. His voice, his eyes, the way he rested his elbow on his knee. Everything.
"You're not him," I said.
My father smiled sadly.
"Of course I am. I'm just in reruns now."
The laugh track came from inside the walls.

The room began to change. The walls opened like curtains. Where the bed had been, a couch appeared. Where the window had been, there was a studio camera. Hot lights lowered from the ceiling. A red sign lit up in front of me:
APPLAUSE.
And something applauded.
I don't know how many of them were in the dark, but I heard hundreds of hands clapping. Some sounded wet. Some sounded too small. Some didn't sound human.
The sitcom father appeared in the doorway.
He no longer looked like an actor. His smile stayed fixed, but the skin around his mouth was tearing from the effort.
"The audience loves a family reunion," he said.

That was when I understood what Channel 9 did. It didn't kill you right away. It offered you company. It showed you what you had lost. It gave you a perfect scene where nobody aged, nobody left, and every pain was resolved before the commercial break.
And once you agreed to sit down, you became part of the show.
Forever.
I remembered my grandmother's notebook.
"Do not watch Channel 9 after midnight."
But on the next page, there was another sentence, one I hadn't understood at first:
"The antenna is the mouth."
I ran toward the attic.

The hallway stretched, as if the house didn't want to let me reach it. Behind every door, I heard a different version of my life: my mother calling my name, my father laughing, my grandmother singing in the kitchen. Warm voices. Impossible voices.
I climbed into the attic and found the antenna cable coming in through the roof.
It wasn't a normal cable.
It was covered in something dark, like burned skin. It pulsed with every burst of laughter coming from the TV downstairs.
I grabbed an old pair of pruning shears.
When I closed them around the cable, every screen in the house turned on at once: the living room TV, the bedroom TV, my phone, even the microwave reflecting an image.
My father appeared on all of them.
"Don't turn me off again," he said.
I almost dropped the shears.
But then I heard another voice.
My grandmother's voice, faint, behind the signal.
"Run, mijo."
I cut the cable.
The house screamed with fake laughter.
A laugh track, repeated, broken, desperate. The TVs exploded. The lights went out. The attic went silent.
At sunrise, the police found me sitting in the yard, covered in dust, clutching my grandmother's notebook. They said lightning had probably struck the antenna, even though there hadn't been a storm.
I sold the house for almost nothing.
The buyer demolished it six months later.
I thought that was the end of it.
But last week, in my apartment in Chicago, my new TV showed a channel that wasn't in the guide.
Channel 9.
There was only static.
Then a living room appeared.
My living room.
On the couch sat my grandmother, calmly knitting.
She looked up and smiled.
"Mijo," she said, "tonight we have a new episode."
Then, from somewhere behind the camera, I heard the applause.
